title.title: Industries & Case Studies
title.subtitle: Where smart contracts are reshaping real industries — and the case studies that prove it (and the ones that didn't)

amm.title: AMM Bonding Curve ·
amm.subtitle: Drag the slider to simulate trading on a Uniswap-style pool. Watch how the curve, prices, and slippage move in real time.
amm.slippageMassive: Massive slippage — your trade is too large for this pool.
amm.slippageNoticeable: Noticeable slippage — typical for medium trades on shallow pools.
amm.slippageMinimal: Minimal slippage — small trade on a deep pool.
amm.invariantC: . Buy more ETH → reserves shrink → price rises hyperbolically. There's no order book — just the curve.

rwaSpectrum.title: Tokenization Spectrum · Easier → Harder
rwaSpectrum.subtitle: Click any asset class to see the complexity factors and current adoption examples.

sov.label: Sovereign Debt
sov.desc: Treasuries, gov bonds. Lowest legal complexity, deepest market, most institutional appetite.
sov.adoption: BlackRock BUIDL, Franklin BENJI, Ondo OUSG

pubeq.label: Public Equities
pubeq.desc: Listed stocks. Need broker-dealer wrapping; SEC restrictions apply.

corpbond.label: Corporate Bonds
corpbond.desc: Issued by companies. Settlement on-chain saves significant cost vs Euroclear/DTCC.
corpbond.adoption: Santander Ethereum bond ($20M, 2019), EIB digital bond

credit.label: Private Credit
credit.desc: Loans to private firms. KYC heavy; profitable but illiquid.

struct.label: Structured Credit
struct.desc: CLOs, CDOs. Complex tranching → hardest to standardize on-chain.

funds.label: Funds & Fund Interests
funds.desc: Tokenized LP/GP interests. Allows fractionalisation of historically illiquid funds.
funds.adoption: Hamilton Lane on Securitize, Apollo digital partnership

commod.label: Commodities
commod.desc: Gold, oil, agricultural. Need custodian + audit infrastructure.

trade.label: Trade Finance
trade.desc: Letters of credit, invoice factoring. Deeply paper-bound; eBL networks help.

realest.label: Real Estate
realest.desc: Physical property. Legal title transfer the hardest part.

infra.label: Infrastructure
infra.desc: Roads, energy, telecom. Long horizons + complex stakeholders.
infra.adoption: Rare on-chain examples; mostly conceptual today

poe.title: Proof of Existence · Try it yourself
poe.subtitle: Hash a credential, anchor it on-chain, then verify it. The blockchain never sees the document — only the hash.
poe.verifyPlaceholderPrompt: Anchor a hash first, then come back here to verify a (possibly tampered) credential.
poe.verifyTextareaPlaceholder: Paste the document you want to verify…
poe.whyWorksBody: SHA-256 is deterministic and collision-resistant. Same input → same hash. Any change to the input — even a single character — produces a completely different hash. Anchoring the hash on-chain proves the document existed at that moment, without revealing its contents.

pegSim.title: Stablecoin Peg Simulator
pegSim.subtitle: Pick a stablecoin design and apply market stress. Watch the peg hold — or break.
pegSim.shockLabel: Market shock — % of supply sold off
pegSim.shockDesc: Sudden sell pressure (a "bank run" event). 100% = entire supply hits the bid simultaneously.
pegSim.noteBody: these formulas are illustrative, not market models. Real peg dynamics involve liquidation cascades, market depth, redemption windows, and reflexive trading. The point:
pegSim.noteEmphasis: each design has a different failure mode

fiat.name: Fiat-backed
fiat.controlDesc: How much of circulating supply is provably backed in a bank/T-bills

crypto.name: Crypto-collateralized
crypto.controlDesc: Value of locked ETH (or other crypto) per $1 minted. 150% = $150 ETH backs $100 DAI

algo.name: Algorithmic
algo.controlDesc: Market-cap of the volatile sister token (LUNA-style) relative to the stablecoin supply

lessons.fiat: Fiat-backed stablecoins are only as trustworthy as their auditor. If reserves dip below 100%, a coordinated sell-off can break the peg — see USDC briefly de-pegging in March 2023 when SVB held $3.3B of its reserves.
lessons.crypto: Crypto-collateralized stablecoins survive shocks via liquidations — but only if the collateral ratio is comfortably above 100%. Drop below that during a flash crash and the protocol can't liquidate fast enough.
lessons.algo: Algorithmic stablecoins are vulnerable to reflexive death spirals: as the stable de-pegs, traders dump the sister token, which collapses, which removes the cushion. UST/LUNA wiped out $40B in May 2022.

royalty.title: Royalty Cascade · ERC-2981 in action
royalty.subtitle: A secondary sale closes. Watch the smart contract pay every party — automatically, on-chain.

provenance.title: Scan a Carrefour BIO Chicken
provenance.subtitle: Click the QR code to retrieve the on-chain provenance. Each step is signed by the party that handled the product.
provenance.handoffsRest: recorded on a permissioned chain. The QR resolves to a content hash — the data itself is on-chain.
provenance.scanPrompt: ← Scan the QR to load the trail

0.label: Hatched
0.detail: Free-range certified · breed: Label Rouge ·  cohort #B-2401

1.label: Raised
1.detail: Organic feed (no GMO, no antibiotics) · 81 days outdoor access

2.label: Transported
2.detail: Refrigerated truck · 3–5 °C maintained · IoT sensor logs attached

4.label: Packaged
4.detail: Carrefour BIO label applied · QR tag printed · batch #BIO-2026-0508

6.label: Stocked
6.detail: Shelf-life: 5 days · price tag generated · scan-to-trace QR live

exercise.title: Design Your Own Smart Contract
exercise.subtitle: Pick an industry, then think through each prompt before revealing the example answer.
exercise.complete: ✓ Brief complete! Now try another industry.

prompts.0: What problem exists in {{industry}} that requires trust in an intermediary?
prompts.1: Who are the 2–3 parties involved? Who holds the funds or assets?
prompts.2: What event or condition triggers the smart contract to execute?
prompts.3: What does the contract do automatically when triggered?
prompts.4: What could go wrong? What is the single weakest point in this design?

Finance.pain: Cross-border payments take 3–5 days and cost 5–8% in fees
Finance.trigger: Sender deposits funds + recipient KYC confirmed
Finance.output: Funds released instantly to recipient in local currency
Finance.risk: Oracle needed for FX rate — potential manipulation

Supply Chain.pain: Invoice disputes between suppliers and retailers take 3–5 weeks to resolve
Supply Chain.trigger: Delivery scan confirmed + invoice matched to PO
Supply Chain.risk: IoT sensor data must come from a trusted oracle

Healthcare.pain: Patient consent for data sharing is paper-based and slow
Healthcare.output: Access token minted — Hospital B can read specific records
Healthcare.risk: Medical data itself must stay off-chain (privacy law)

Real Estate.pain: Property title transfer takes 30–60 days and costs 6% in intermediary fees
Real Estate.trigger: Full payment received + title search clear
Real Estate.output: Deed NFT transferred to buyer; funds released to seller
Real Estate.risk: Legal recognition of NFT as deed requires legislation

Government.pain: Benefit disbursements are slow and subject to fraud
Government.trigger: Eligibility criteria confirmed via verified credentials
Government.output: Benefit amount sent directly to citizen wallet
Government.risk: Off-chain identity verification is the weak point

Entertainment.pain: Music royalties take 18 months to reach artists through label chains
Entertainment.risk: High transaction volume — requires L2 or rollup

Insurance.pain: Flight delay claims require manual submission and 2–4 week payout
Insurance.output: Fixed payout sent automatically to traveller wallet
Insurance.risk: Oracle data quality is the single point of failure

Gaming.pain: In-game assets can be deleted or altered by the developer at any time
Gaming.trigger: Player purchases or earns item in-game
Gaming.output: NFT minted to player wallet — tradeable and permanent
Gaming.risk: Game server still centralised; contract only covers ownership

teamPick.title: Pick your industry
teamPick.subtitle: The next five chapters walk through DeFi, Gaming/NFTs, RWA, Supply Chain, and Digital Certification — pick the one your team's problem statement lives in, so you can study it with intent.

have.item1B: your team agreed on at the end of Day 2.
have.item2: Some sense of which industry it touches (banking? retail? logistics? government?).

discuss.item1: Which of the five upcoming verticals fits your problem best?
discuss.item2A: If multiple fit, pick the one where the smart-contract pattern is
discuss.item3: If none fit cleanly, that's a signal worth noting — your problem may need adapting (or may not be a great smart-contract fit).

during.item1: Pay extra attention to the vertical you chose — note the existing players, the real-world deployments, and the pros/cons.
during.item2: The other verticals still matter — patterns transfer. Capture anything that looks adjacent to your problem.
during.item3B: later in the day is where you'll formally test if blockchain is the right tool.

industriesIntro.heading: Smart Contracts Across Industries
industriesIntro.subtitle: Five verticals where smart contracts have moved from theory to deployed production systems.

nft.name: Gaming & NFTs
nft.desc: True ownership, P2E economies, embedded royalties

industriesIntro.commonThreadBody: in every vertical below, smart contracts replace a slow, intermediary-dependent process with code-enforced execution. We'll examine each, then look at five flagship case studies — including two that failed.

defiDivider.title: Decentralized Finance
defiDivider.subtitle: Blockchain-based financial applications that operate without banks or brokers — peer-to-peer transactions enforced by smart contracts.

defiWhat.heading: What is DeFi?
defiWhat.subtitle: An emerging peer-to-peer financial system using cryptocurrencies and smart contracts.
defiWhat.coreIdeaBody: Remove third-party intermediaries. Instead of a bank enforcing a loan or a clearinghouse settling a trade, a smart contract automatically executes those functions when conditions are met. Cutting out middlemen reduces fees, settlement times, and gives users more control over their assets.
defiWhat.valueCodeTitle: "Value and Code at the same level"
defiWhat.valueCodeBody: In traditional finance, the rules of trade live in legal documents and back-office systems separate from the money itself. In DeFi, value (ETH, USDC, NFTs) and the rules that govern it (the contract code) sit on the same blockchain — composable, atomic, transparent.

dex.name: DEXs / AMMs
dex.desc: Decentralized exchanges using liquidity pools (Uniswap, CoW Swap, Curve)

lending.name: Lending
lending.desc: Over-collateralized loans without credit checks (Aave, Compound)

stablecoins.name: Stablecoins
stablecoins.desc: Tokens pegged to USD/EUR (USDC, DAI, USDT) — global, programmable money

yield.name: Yield
yield.desc: Automated strategies that route deposits to highest-return pools (Yearn)

derivatives.name: Derivatives
derivatives.desc: Perpetuals, options, synthetics — trading without intermediaries (dYdX, GMX)

defiDex.heading: Decentralized Exchanges (DEXs)
defiDex.subtitle: Trading venues executed entirely by smart contracts — no central broker, no clearing house, no custody risk.
defiDex.uniswapBody: Lets any wallet swap thousands of tokens in one on-chain call. Liquidity providers deposit token pairs; the smart contract's AMM quotes the rate, moves the assets, and charges 0.01–1% per pool, settling in about 15 seconds.
defiDex.cowBodyB: rather than on-chain swap transactions. Off-chain solvers batch orders, match users directly through Coincidence of Wants, and route any leftover flow to on-chain liquidity. Trades settle in a single transaction with built-in MEV protection.
defiDex.whyMattersBody: a DEX delivers centralized-exchange-scale liquidity without ever taking custody of user funds. The contract enforces the rules; users keep their keys.

stablecoinsIntro.heading: Stablecoins — Crypto That Holds $1
stablecoinsIntro.subtitle: The most-used on-chain asset: tokens engineered to track a stable reference, almost always 1 USD. Three designs, three failure modes.

fiat.name: Fiat-backed
fiat.how: A company holds real dollars / T-bills in a bank and issues one token per dollar.
fiat.risk: You must trust the issuer and its reserves.

crypto.name: Crypto-collateralised
crypto.how: Users lock surplus crypto (e.g. $150 of ETH) in a contract to mint $100 of stablecoin.
crypto.risk: A sharp crash can under-collateralise and trigger liquidations.

algo.name: Algorithmic
algo.how: Code expands and contracts supply to chase the peg — no real assets behind it.
algo.risk: Reflexive: confidence loss spirals. UST/LUNA wiped out ~$40B in 2022.

stablecoinsIntro.nextBody: stress-test a peg yourself — push collateral and demand until a stablecoin breaks.

borderless.imgAlt: A laptop with a shield, a wallet, and a secure cloud — borderless value transfer
borderless.heading: Borderless Value Transfers
borderless.subtitle: Moving digital assets entirely by smart contract — no correspondent banks, no clearing networks.
tradeoffsItems: Wallet UX: seed phrases, gas, approvals

pros.label: 24/7 Global Access
pros.desc: Markets, payments, and dollar-pegged balances stay open all day — no cut-off windows or borders.

pros.label: Instant Settlement
pros.desc: Trades and transfers clear in a single block (seconds), eliminating clearing delays.

pros.label: Reduced Costs
pros.desc: Smart contracts replace banks, card networks, and brokers — fees on swaps and cross-border payments drop dramatically.

pros.label: Self-Custody
pros.desc: Users keep their own keys. Value moves peer-to-peer without frozen accounts or withdrawal limits.

pros.label: Predictable Value
pros.desc: Stablecoins let users hold and spend a token that maintains a fiat parity (mostly USD).

pros.label: Programmable Cash Flows
pros.desc: Code automates swaps, payouts, and recurring transfers once preset conditions are met.

pros.label: Permissionless Markets
pros.desc: Anyone can list and launch a new token pair or liquidity pool. No gatekeepers.

cons.label: Regulatory Uncertainty
cons.desc: Rules on KYC, securities status, and stablecoin backing differ by jurisdiction and keep evolving.

cons.label: Smart Contract Bugs
cons.desc: Technical vulnerabilities or exploits can drain pools, freeze funds, or break a stablecoin peg.

cons.label: Liquidity Fragmentation
cons.desc: Assets spread across many chains and pools. Big trades face slippage or thin depth.

cons.label: Peg Instability
cons.desc: Even "stable" coins can de-peg if reserves, collateral ratios, or algorithms fail.

cons.label: UX Complexity
cons.desc: Setting up wallets, safeguarding seed phrases, paying gas — all deter mainstream adoption.

cons.label: On/Off-Ramp Friction
cons.desc: Moving funds between banks and blockchain wallets still depends on centralized exchanges and KYC.

nftDivider.title: Gaming & NFTs
nftDivider.subtitle: Non-Fungible Tokens turn digital items into player-owned assets — with embedded royalties and tradeable secondary markets.

nftIntro.heading: What is an NFT?
nftIntro.subtitle: A unique digital asset recorded on-chain that certifies ownership and authenticity.
nftIntro.unlikeBodyB: — your 1 ETH equals my 1 ETH. NFTs are not. Each NFT has its own ID and metadata, recorded on the ERC-721 (or ERC-1155) standard. Owning the token means owning that specific asset.
nftIntro.gamingBody: NFTs can represent swords, skins, characters, or virtual land. Because the NFT sits in the player's Web3 wallet — not on the publisher's server — it can be listed, traded, or rented peer-to-peer. Open, player-controlled markets emerge.

nftEconomies.heading: True Ownership, Digital IP & Virtual Economies
nftEconomies.subtitle: Smart contracts turn server-side database rows into player-owned, transferable, programmable assets.

before.title: Before Web3
points: Digital assets lived on centralized servers, owned by publishers
Account banned = items gone, no recourse
Creators received zero royalties on resales

withNft.title: With NFTs
points: Asset ownership lives in the user's wallet on-chain
Embedded royalties pay creators on every secondary sale

parallels.title: Real-world parallels
points: Sell items for real-world value (just like physical goods)
Lend NFTs out for yield (like real-estate rental)
Use NFTs as collateral for loans (mortgaging digital art)
Sell fractional shares (REITs for digital assets)

p2eReal.imgAlt: Play-to-Earn ecosystem icons: gaming, NFTs, tokens, creatures
p2eReal.heading: Play-to-Earn in Production
p2eReal.subtitle: Smart contracts link in-game achievements to token rewards — and route perpetual royalties back to creators on every resale.
howPoints: Win a match, breed a creature, finish a quest → smart contract mints/transfers token rewards to your wallet
Tokens swappable for stablecoins or reinvested in better gear
Guilds emerge — NFT holders lend assets to new players for revenue split
Labor and capital combine inside a self-governing digital economy

ubisoftPoints.b: minted in a capped drop — sold out in hours, funding live-ops upfront

ubisoftPoints.a: Players battle squads in PvP; victories earn on-chain reward tokens redeemable for gear

ubisoftPoints.b: to Ubisoft and collaborating artists — perpetual revenue loop

p2eReal.ubisoftFootnote: A mainstream AAA studio using NFTs and smart-contract royalties to power an active game economy in 2025.

nftFinance.heading: NFT Finance · Lending, Renting, Yield
nftFinance.subtitle: If digital assets have value, secondary liquidity can be built around them.
nftFinance.lendingBody: NFT holders borrow against their assets without selling. The NFT is locked in a smart contract; if the loan defaults, the contract auctions or transfers the NFT to the lender. Liquidity unlocked, ownership preserved.
nftFinance.rentalsBody: NFTs can be rented to new players for a revenue split. As assets circulate, utilization grows, lenders earn yield secured by on-chain collateral, liquidity deepens, better pricing emerges, attracting more capital — a self-reinforcing flywheel.
nftFinance.rentalsPattern: Pattern: borrow → use in-game → earn → split with owner
nftFinance.zhartaImgAlt: Zharta loan volume — Borrow chart over a year, +300 loans, 13.6% APR
nftFinance.zhartaBodyA: Borrow against your NFT — keep 100% upside, retain investing opportunities, share the risk. Zharta's smart contracts grew to
nftFinance.zhartaBodyC: , with 2-year terms — secured by on-chain collateral.

pros.label: True Digital Ownership
pros.desc: Items live in the player's wallet, can't be revoked, and survive server shutdowns.

pros.label: Player-Driven Economies
pros.desc: Open on-chain markets set prices; supply & demand determine value, not publishers.

pros.label: Programmable Cash Flows
pros.desc: Smart contracts automate rentals, revenue-sharing, staking, and collateralized lending.

pros.label: Perpetual Creator Royalties
pros.desc: Each secondary sale auto-routes a percentage back to devs and artists for continuous income.

pros.label: Transparent Scarcity
pros.desc: Mint caps and supply are public on-chain — preventing hidden inflation of rare items.

pros.label: Global 24/7 Markets
pros.desc: Anyone with a wallet can buy, sell, or earn rewards instantly across borders.

pros.label: Composability
pros.desc: NFTs plug into DeFi or social dApps, unlocking governance, yield, or utility bonuses.

cons.label: Price Volatility
cons.desc: Item and token values can spike or crash — disrupting game balance and player trust.

cons.label: Regulatory Gray Zones
cons.desc: Loot-box-style drops and token rewards may face gambling or securities scrutiny.

cons.label: UX Friction
cons.desc: Wallet setup, key management, and gas fees are barriers for mainstream gamers.

cons.label: Scams & Counterfeits
cons.desc: Phishing sites and fake collections trick players into signing malicious transactions.

cons.label: Game-Health Dependency
cons.desc: Asset value hinges on an active player base. If the game fades, prices collapse.

cons.label: Market Saturation
cons.desc: A flood of low-quality drops dilutes attention and depresses resale demand.

rwaDivider.title: Real Asset Tokenization
rwaDivider.subtitle: Converting ownership rights in physical or financial assets into digital tokens — automated by smart contracts.

rwaSecuritize.heading: Securitize · BlackRock BUIDL · BNY Mellon
rwaSecuritize.subtitle: A regulated digital asset issuance and management platform bridging TradFi and blockchain.
howPoints: Financial firms issue digital securities on blockchain networks (Ethereum, Polygon, Avalanche)
Each token represents ownership of off-chain assets — treasuries, private credit, structured products
Investors receive tokenized shares, tradeable and settleable on-chain
KYC/AML automated via on-chain compliance modules
rwaSecuritize.regulatoryBody: Registered with SEC/FINRA — supports compliant digital securities issuance under U.S. law.
rwaSecuritize.buidlBody: USD Institutional Digital Liquidity Fund — tokenized money-market exposure for institutions.
rwaSecuritize.bnyBody: Tokenized fund backed by AAA-rated structured credit — institutional credit exposure on-chain.

rwaMortgages.heading: Automated Mortgages
rwaMortgages.subtitle: Lending agreements executed entirely by smart contracts — no banks or loan officers required.
contractPoints: Holds and transfers funds based on pre-coded conditions
Monitors repayment schedule — calculates interest, principal split
Applies penalties on late payments automatically
Manages collateral — liquidates if loan defaults
whyPoints: Transparent terms — borrower and lender see the same code
Lower cost — no bank back office
Programmable repayments — dynamic interest, deferrals encoded
rwaMortgages.limitationBody: the property itself still requires legal title transfer off-chain — for now.

rwaCollateral.heading: RWA Collateral Lending
rwaCollateral.subtitle: Borrowing against tokenized real-world assets without selling them.

cards.title: Capital efficiency
cards.desc: Investors borrow against tokenized securities without selling — keeping market exposure while unlocking liquidity.

cards.title: Programmable collateral
cards.desc: On-chain securities enable real-time valuation, automated margining, and instant collateral management.

cards.title: Lower lender risk
cards.desc: Loans secured by regulated, price-discoverable assets with enforceable claims under existing law.

cards.title: Faster settlement
cards.desc: Margin calls, top-ups, and liquidations execute instantly — no T+1 or T+2 delay.

cards.title: Scalable credit layer
cards.desc: A compliant on-chain securities financing market — bridging TradFi and DeFi.

cards.title: New investor classes
cards.desc: Retail investors gain access to instruments historically reserved for institutions.

rwaProperty.heading: Property Sales · Real Pilots
rwaProperty.subtitle: Property transactions involve escrow, title, notaries — and weeks. Blockchain pilots compress this dramatically.

sweden.title: Land Registry Pilot
sweden.bodyA: Private blockchain for property conveyance involving banks and government agencies. Title registration dropped from
sweden.bodyB: . All parties access the same tamper-proof records; signing and title updates done digitally.

frankfurt.title: Brickblock
frankfurt.bodyA: Blockchain-based real-estate investment platform. Built on
frankfurt.bodyB: . Property tokens issued under German regulation.

switzerland.title: Zurich Bahnhofstrasse Building
switzerland.body: Tokenization of a prestigious building on Zurich's most expensive street. Ownership shares issued as digital tokens via a Swiss GmbH structure. Investors receive rental income and trade tokens on a secondary market.

rwaProperty.micaBody: the first comprehensive regulatory framework for crypto-assets across the EU — providing legal clarity, consumer protection, and market stability across all 27 member states.

pros.label: Fractional Ownership
pros.desc: Small investors gain access to high-value real estate, art, or institutional funds with low capital.

pros.label: Increased Liquidity
pros.desc: Tokenized assets can trade on secondary markets — improving exit options for historically illiquid holdings.

pros.label: 24/7 Global Access
pros.desc: Investors participate from anywhere, any time — no broker hours, no geographic gating.

pros.label: Reduced Costs
pros.desc: Smart contracts automate compliance, transfer, and payout workflows — slashing administrative overhead.

pros.label: Transparent Records
pros.desc: Blockchain ensures tamper-proof title, transaction history, and audit trails.

pros.label: Programmable Cash Flows
pros.desc: Rental income, dividends, coupons distribute automatically via smart contracts.

pros.label: Faster Settlement
pros.desc: Property transfers and securities settlement compress from weeks to minutes/days.

cons.label: Regulatory Uncertainty
cons.desc: Rules vary by country and evolve quickly — compliance can become a moving target.

cons.label: Low Market Maturity
cons.desc: Few active platforms; secondary market volume remains thin compared to traditional venues.

cons.label: Technical Complexity
cons.desc: Investors need to understand wallets, smart contracts, custody — UX is not consumer-grade yet.

cons.label: Cybersecurity Risk
cons.desc: Vulnerabilities in smart contracts or platforms can expose investors to total loss.

cons.label: Lingering Illiquidity
cons.desc: Despite tokenization, some real-estate assets simply lack buyers — wrappers don't create demand.

cons.label: Custodial Wrappers Required
cons.desc: Real-world assets still need legal entities (SPVs, trustees) to hold underlying property.

cons.label: Valuation Drift
cons.desc: On-chain token prices may diverge from underlying asset NAV — pricing reliability is an open problem.

scDivider.title: Supply Chain
scDivider.subtitle: Recording every hand-off of a physical good — from raw-material pickup to retail shelf — as a tamper-proof block.

scProvReal.heading: Provenance in Production
scProvReal.subtitle: Two flagship deployments — luxury anti-counterfeit and organic-food traceability.
scProvReal.lvmhBodyA: Every handbag, watch, or perfume bottle gets a
scProvReal.lvmhBodyB: on a permissioned blockchain — recording materials, manufacturing date, and ownership changes. Scanning the embedded NFC or QR tag reveals provenance to shoppers, while smart-contract updates ensure resale platforms and after-sales services access accurate, brand-verified data throughout the product's lifetime.
scProvReal.lvmhGoalBody: fight counterfeiting and enhance customer trust across luxury brands.
scProvReal.carrefourBody: Every Carrefour BIO chicken carries a QR code. Scanning pulls a blockchain record showing breed, farm GPS, organic feed, antibiotic-free status, slaughter date, packing plant, and temperature-controlled transport history. Data uploaded by farmers, processors, and logistics partners at hand-off — locked on a permissioned ledger.
scProvReal.carrefourFootnote: Faster recalls, stronger trust in the organic label, instant tamper-proof proof of authenticity.

scShipping.heading: Global Shipping Documents · GSBN eBL
scShipping.subtitle: Bills of Lading and customs forms, courier-couriered for centuries — now tokenized on a shared ledger.
legacyPoints: Bills of Lading and Letters of Credit move as paper — couriered across oceans
Documents prone to loss, fraud, week-long delays
Carriers, ports, banks, and regulators each maintain separate records
Reconciling discrepancies takes days of phone calls
scShipping.gsbnImgAlt: Container ship and an electronic Bill of Lading with QR code
scShipping.gsbnTag: COSCO · Hapag-Lloyd · OOCL · ONE + major ports

gsbnPoints.a: Permissioned blockchain issuing electronic Bills of Lading (eBLs)
Each digital document cryptographically signed, shareable in seconds, tamper-evident
Smart contracts notify customs on cargo load, trigger goods release on ownership transfer, settle carrier fees

pros.label: Transparency
pros.desc: All partners see the same tamper-proof ledger — eliminating information asymmetry.

pros.label: Real-Time Traceability
pros.desc: A product's full journey can be located within seconds — not days.

pros.label: Process Automation
pros.desc: Smart contracts trigger payments, releases, and alerts without manual checks.

pros.label: Faster Dispute Resolution
pros.desc: Shared data removes he-said-she-said — investigations and chargebacks shorten dramatically.

pros.label: Cost Reduction
pros.desc: Less paperwork, fewer courier fees, streamlined audits — overall overhead drops.

pros.label: Regulatory Compliance
pros.desc: Immutable logs provide instant evidence for food, pharma, and customs inspections.

pros.label: Consumer Trust
pros.desc: Shoppers scan a code to verify origin, quality, and handling conditions.

cons.label: Data-Input Integrity
cons.desc: "Garbage in, garbage forever" — bad sensor data still writes immutably to the chain.

cons.label: Integration Complexity
cons.desc: Legacy ERPs, IoT devices, and port systems require costly and complicated connections.

cons.label: Standard Fragmentation
cons.desc: Competing consortia hinder network effects and global interoperability.

cons.label: Implementation Cost
cons.desc: Sensors, middleware, and change management demand significant upfront investment.

cons.label: Scalability
cons.desc: Public chains may struggle with container-level transaction volumes.

cons.label: Stakeholder Coordination
cons.desc: Rival companies must cooperate. Adoption stalls if key partners refuse to participate.

certDivider.title: Digital Certification
certDivider.subtitle: Tamper-proof, easily verifiable digital records — diplomas, land titles, product authenticity — issued and validated on-chain.

certLaw.heading: Estonia · KSI Blockchain (Guardtime)
certLaw.subtitle: Government records, registries, and healthcare data integrity since 2012.

whatPoints.a: Enforces integrity of land registry, business registry, court system, healthcare records
"Keyless" signatures — relies on hash chains, not private keys
Detects any tampering of historical records

certLaw.principleQuote: "History cannot be rewritten by anybody and the authenticity of electronic data can be guaranteed."
certLaw.principleBody: By using blockchain as a backbone for data integrity, Estonia ensures official records are beyond tampering — even by future government insiders. This is digital sovereignty by mathematical proof rather than institutional trust.
certLaw.whyBodyB: — not a product. The same principle now underpins their e-Residency program and X-Road data exchange.

certEducation.heading: Education · MIT Blockcerts
certEducation.subtitle: Combating fake degrees and simplifying credential verification.
problemPoints: Verifying a diploma traditionally requires emailing the registrar — days of delay
Fake transcripts and credentials are a multi-billion-dollar fraud market
International credential recognition is especially friction-heavy
Students don't truly own their academic record — institutions do

mitPoints.a: App lets graduates share a tamper-proof, verifiable digital diploma
Employers click a link or scan a QR code → instantly verify on-chain

certEducation.footnote: Open standard now adopted by universities globally — students truly own their credentials.

certProducts.heading: Product Authenticity · Luxury Goods
certProducts.subtitle: Digital "title deeds" of authenticity for high-value physical products.
certProducts.howBody: When a customer buys a high-end handbag, watch, or piece of jewelry, they scan a code that retrieves the item's blockchain certificate. The certificate includes origin, materials, manufacturing date, and full ownership history.
howPoints: After-sales service tied to verified ownership
certProducts.auraBody: Co-founded by LVMH (Louis Vuitton, Hublot, Bulgari, etc.) alongside Prada and Cartier. Issues a unique digital certificate for each luxury item — handbag, watch, jewelry — recorded on a secure blockchain. Consumers scan to access authenticity, provenance, and ownership history.
certProducts.auraFootnote: Industry-wide standard for luxury authentication — collaborative rather than competitive infrastructure.

pros.label: Tamper-Proof & Verifiable
pros.desc: Certificates are hashed and stored on-chain — making them immutable.

pros.label: Eliminates Forgery
pros.desc: Even single-character alterations are detectable. Reduces fake diplomas, licenses, and credentials to ~zero.

pros.label: No Intermediaries
pros.desc: Removes the need for notaries, registry offices, or institutional verification emails.

pros.label: Instant & Global
pros.desc: Recipients share credentials digitally; employers verify them in seconds, anywhere.

pros.label: Cost & Time Efficiency
pros.desc: No more printing, postage, manual checks. Issuance and verification accelerate dramatically.

pros.label: Self-Sovereignty
pros.desc: Users hold their own credentials — reducing institutional gatekeeping.

cons.label: Legal Recognition
cons.desc: In many countries, blockchain-based records aren't legally recognized as official proof.

cons.label: User Literacy
cons.desc: Users need basic knowledge of blockchain, wallets, or verification tools to participate.

cons.label: Implementation Cost
cons.desc: Setting up a robust certification system requires technical expertise and initial investment.

cons.label: Privacy Concerns
cons.desc: Even hashed metadata may leak personal information if not carefully designed.

cons.label: Revocation Complexity
cons.desc: Once written to a blockchain, it's permanent. Revoking or updating requires extra contract logic.

cons.label: Vendor Lock-in
cons.desc: Different consortia use different chains — interoperability still emerging.

casesDivider.title: Cross-Industry Case Studies
casesDivider.subtitle: Eight flagship deployments — five successes, two cautionary tales, one notorious hack. Real lessons for real builders.

homedepot.heading: Home Depot: Supply Chain Dispute Resolution
homedepot.subtitle: How the world's largest home improvement retailer used blockchain to eliminate weeks of supplier disputes.
homedepot.problemBodyB: across a global supply chain. When goods arrived damaged, delayed, or incorrect, resolving the dispute required:
problemPoints: Weeks of back-and-forth emails between buyer and supplier
Manual reconciliation of paper invoices, purchase orders, and shipping docs
No single source of truth — each party had different records
Average resolution time: 3–5 weeks, tying up cash flow
homedepot.problemCostBody: Millions in delayed payments, admin overhead, and strained supplier relationships
solutionPoints: All purchase orders, shipping confirmations, and invoices recorded on a shared ledger in real time
Smart contracts auto-match delivery confirmations against POs — flagging discrepancies instantly
Both Home Depot and suppliers see the same immutable data — no he-said-she-said
Dispute resolution triggers automatically when conditions deviate from contract terms
homedepot.solutionFlow: Delivery confirmed → Invoice auto-approved → Payment released
homedepot.platformBody: IBM Blockchain (Hyperledger Fabric) — permissioned, only Home Depot and its suppliers participate.

propy.heading: Propy: Real-Estate NFT Deeds
propy.subtitle: The first legally recognised property sale via NFT — and what it means for real estate globally.
propy.problemBodyA: Real estate transactions remain one of the most
problemPoints: 30–60 day closing timelines: title search, escrow, notarization, county recording
Multiple intermediaries: agents, lawyers, title companies, banks — each taking a cut
Paper-based deeds stored in local county offices — vulnerable to loss, fraud, and errors
Cross-border property purchases nearly impossible without local legal representation
propy.problemCostBody: $1.7T in annual home sales — ~6% in transaction costs
propy.solutionBodyA: Propy places the property deed inside an
propy.solutionBodyB: (Ethereum). Ownership of the NFT = legal ownership of the property:
solutionPoints: Property is held inside an LLC; the NFT represents ownership of the LLC
Transferring the NFT = transferring the LLC = transferring the property
Smart contract handles escrow — funds released only when title is confirmed
Transaction recorded on Ethereum: public, permanent, globally accessible
Settlement time: hours instead of weeks
propy.solutionFlow: NFT transfer → LLC transfer → Property transfer

results.label: First NFT property sale — Kyiv, Ukraine (2017)
First US NFT home sale — Gulfport, Florida (2022)
Any buyer, anywhere — no local agent required

propy.caveatBody: legal framework still requires the LLC wrapper. Direct deed-as-NFT requires legislative change in most jurisdictions.

dmv.heading: California DMV: Vehicle Titles on Avalanche
dmv.subtitle: The largest US state DMV moves 42 million vehicle titles to a public blockchain — live in 2024.
dmv.problemBodyB: on legacy paper-based systems dating back decades:
problemPoints: Paper titles easily lost, damaged, or forged — enabling vehicle fraud worth billions annually
Title transfers require in-person DMV visits — average wait: 2–3 hours
Lenders cannot instantly verify clear title before approving auto loans
Out-of-state transfers require duplicate paperwork and weeks of processing
dmv.problemScaleBody: ~42M titles, largest state vehicle registry in the US
dmv.solutionBodyB: to migrate all CA vehicle titles to the
solutionPoints: Each vehicle title becomes a unique NFT — representing legal ownership on-chain
Title transfers executed as blockchain transactions — instant, tamper-proof, auditable
Lienholders (banks) can verify and release liens digitally — no paper, no delay
Avalanche chosen for: EVM compatibility, low fees, high throughput, and custom subnet support
DMV retains control via a permissioned Avalanche subnet
dmv.solutionFlow: Title NFT minted → Transfer = blockchain tx → Lien released on-chain

results.label: State government blockchain title system at scale

dmv.takeawayBody: even government institutions trust blockchain for legal record-keeping — when the use case (immutable, auditable, shared ownership) fits perfectly.

walmart.imgAlt: Walmart Food Trust — IBM Blockchain on a phone next to a crate of oranges
walmart.heading: Walmart · Food Trust
walmart.subtitle: Tracing food contamination from 7 days to 2.2 seconds — IBM Hyperledger Fabric in production.
walmart.problemBodyA: Food contamination outbreaks (E. coli in lettuce, 2018) took
walmart.problemBodyB: to trace back to source. Entire product categories pulled from shelves. Massive collateral damage to suppliers.
problemPoints: Manual paper trails through farms, distributors, retailers
Multiple parties, each with isolated systems
Trust gaps — who's accountable for contamination?
walmart.solutionBodyEnd: . Every step recorded on a permissioned blockchain — from farm to shelf. All participants see the same data.
solutionPoints: Farmers, packers, distributors, stores all submit data at hand-off
QR codes link physical products to on-chain provenance
Permissioned chain — only network members can read/write
walmart.whyBody: high-value transparency, multi-party trust gap, and a network with strong incentives to participate.

santander.heading: Santander · End-to-End Blockchain Bond
santander.subtitle: $20M bond issued entirely on Ethereum — the first major bank to settle a security on a public chain.

backgroundPoints.a: Entirely on Ethereum mainnet — public, permissionless
First major bank to issue a security on a public blockchain
Cash leg also tokenized — full end-to-end on-chain settlement

santander.backgroundQuote: "A technology innovation issue more than a pure financial issue."
whyPoints: Multiple jurisdictions involved — public chain neutralizes location
Complex traditional settlement → atomic on-chain settlement
Immutability creates a permanent audit trail for regulators
Value of fast settlement > the gas cost

quiz.question: The California DMV digitized 42 million vehicle titles on blockchain. What is the primary advantage of blockchain over the traditional paper title system?
options: Blockchain storage is cheaper than paper filing, reducing the DMV operating budget significantly.
Title transfers and ownership verification can occur in minutes without contacting the DMV — eliminating fraud via immutable provenance records.
Vehicle titles are now publicly visible to anyone without identity verification, increasing transparency.
Vehicle theft becomes impossible because titles are stored on-chain and cannot be cloned.
quiz.explanation: The core blockchain advantage here is trustless verification of ownership history. Traditional paper titles require physical transfer, manual verification, and weeks of processing. With blockchain, a buyer can instantly verify the full ownership chain, detect odometer fraud, and confirm the seller has clear title — all without trusting the DMV database or the seller's word. The immutability makes forgery structurally impossible rather than merely difficult. Blockchain storage is actually more expensive than paper — the value is in verification speed and fraud elimination, not cost.

takeaways.title: Section 03 — Key Takeaways
items: Five industry verticals — DeFi, Gaming/NFTs, RWA, Supply Chain, Digital Cert — all show smart contracts in production today
DeFi: AMMs, stablecoins, and borderless transfers reshape financial rails (Uniswap $1.5B/day, Shopify USDC)
NFTs deliver true digital ownership and embedded creator royalties — Ubisoft Champions Tactics is mainstream
RWA tokenization spans the spectrum from sovereign debt (BlackRock BUIDL) to real estate (Sweden, Brickblock, Zurich)
Supply chain wins when multiple parties need shared truth — Walmart Food Trust traces back from days to seconds
Digital certification works at scale — MIT Blockcerts diplomas (2017+), LVMH luxury authentication
The common thread for success: many parties, no trusted authority, value from automation and shared truth
Coming up in Section 4 — three lessons from the field: when blockchain wins (Estonia), when it backfires (The DAO), when it shouldn't have been picked (ASX)
